title: The Science Behind Next-Best Action
gist: BCG argues that agent-native next-best-action (NBA) marketing systems require running three additive decisioning layers simultaneously—propensity/uplift scoring, contextual bandits, and foundation-model agents—atop a two-track batch/real-time architecture, with the building blocks already deployable but most organizations unstaffed for the required reinforcement-learning and causal-inference work.

insights.claim: Effective next-best-action systems should run three additive layers simultaneously—propensity/uplift scoring, contextual bandits, and foundation-model agents—because each handles a fundamentally different type of intelligence rather than superseding the prior layer.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Vendors or in-house teams selling NBA as a single-model upgrade are mis-framed; the durable architecture is layered and composable.
insights.quote: These layers are additive, not alternative.

insights.claim: The most widely deployed enterprise NBA platforms today operate only at Layer 1, using gradient-boosted trees such as XGBoost or LightGBM or logistic regressions trained on historical response data to score customers on likelihood to respond.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Incumbent martech vendors are technologically behind the agent-native frontier, creating a competitive opening.
insights.quote: The most widely deployed enterprise NBA platforms operate at this layer.

insights.claim: In most NBA contexts Thompson sampling outperforms simpler exploration approaches such as epsilon-greedy because it naturally adapts its exploration rate to uncertainty, exploring more where it knows less and exploiting more where it is confident.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: A concrete algorithmic spec for evaluating which bandit vendors are using best-practice methods.

insights.claim: Foundation-model agents add value where structured models hit a ceiling—interpreting unstructured context such as the tone of a service chat or whether a browsing sequence suggests confusion versus comparison shopping—and decide when to defer to or override the bandit's quantitative recommendation.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: LLMs in marketing stacks are most valuable as the reasoning/override layer, not just content generation.
insights.quote: These are judgment calls, not optimization problems.

insights.claim: No vendor has yet deployed the full agent-native NBA vision at scale, with the first platform bets on the foundation-model reasoning layer only now emerging.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Early-mover window remains open for martech vendors and enterprises building Layer 3 capabilities.
insights.quote: The first platform bets on Layer 3 are emerging, but no vendor has deployed the full vision at scale yet.

themes.theme: Limits of propensity scoring and the uplift shift

insights.claim: Organizations that shift from propensity to uplift modeling typically discover that 20% to 40% of their active marketing programs deliver hardly any incremental lift, instead just capturing intent that would have materialized anyway.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: A large, quantifiable margin leak hiding inside marketing budgets that auditing uplift can recover.
insights.quote: Organizations that make this shift typically discover that 20% to 40% of their active programs deliver hardly any incremental lift
insights.locator: Limitations of Propensity and Uplift Models

insights.claim: Targeting a customer with a 90% propensity to purchase—who may have planned to buy anyway—with a discount destroys margin without changing behavior, which is why uplift modeling that estimates the incremental effect versus no action is the critical Layer 1 upgrade.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Frames discounting waste as a measurable causal-inference problem solvable with T-learners, S-learners, or doubly robust learners.
insights.quote: A customer with a 90% propensity to purchase may have planned to buy anyway; if so, targeting that customer with a discount destroys margin without changing behavior.
insights.locator: Limitations of Propensity and Uplift Models

insights.claim: Propensity and uplift models cannot discover novel action combinations because they only rank within a predefined action space, a binding constraint as the composable shelf grows to hundreds of atomic assets linkable in thousands of combinations.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Justifies investment in bandits and agents once asset libraries scale beyond a handful of fixed offers.

insights.claim: Agent-native NBA requires a two-track architecture separating a 24-to-48-hour batch optimization layer—which computes policies, retrains bandits, and solves portfolio-level allocation—from a real-time serving layer that adapts those policies under roughly 100 milliseconds of latency.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: A concrete systems blueprint to evaluate whether an NBA implementation can actually scale.

insights.claim: Without portfolio-level optimization the real-time layer assigns the best action to each individual customer, quickly exhausting high-value offers on easy-to-convert customers while starving harder-to-reach segments.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Explains why per-customer optimization without budget guardrails produces globally suboptimal spend.
insights.quote: quickly exhausting high-value offers on easy-to-convert customers while starving harder-to-reach segments

insights.claim: Portfolio optimization is typically formulated as a mixed-integer linear program for tractable sizes, using Lagrangian relaxation to decompose the problem when the customer-action matrix exceeds solver limits, and most organizations need both because each alone introduces bias.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Specific algorithmic requirements for vetting whether a system can coordinate allocation at population scale.

insights.claim: Optimizing a bandit for the wrong proxy reward produces a policy that performs well on the proxy metric but fails to move the actual business outcome, making the causal link between proxy and ultimate objective one of the most consequential design decisions in the system.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Reward-metric misalignment is the key hidden failure mode to probe in any RL-based marketing system.
insights.quote: optimizing for the wrong reward will produce a policy that seems to perform well on the proxy metric but fails to move the outcome that actually matters
insights.locator: Objective Function for Reinforcement Learning and the Bandit Layer

insights.claim: Value-based RL methods are the right starting point for most NBA deployments because they work well with constrained action spaces under 1,000 discrete actions, while policy gradient methods suit continuous actions like discount percentage at the cost of needing more exploration data to converge.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Practical default that reduces wasted exploration budget and speeds time-to-value for first bandit builds.
insights.locator: Objective Function for Reinforcement Learning and the Bandit Layer

insights.claim: Pairing a foundation model's reasoned prior with a contextual bandit's controlled exploration lets a system converge on a performance estimate for a new offer within days rather than the weeks or quarters required by manual test-and-learn.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Faster action onboarding is a direct competitive advantage in responding to market shifts.
insights.quote: the system converges on a performance estimate within days rather than quarters
insights.locator: The Cold-Start Problem and Intelligent Exploration

insights.claim: The barrier to agent-native NBA is organizational, not scientific—the techniques are deployable today, but most marketing analytics teams are not staffed for reinforcement learning or bandit optimization and most organizations have not built the two-track architecture.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Talent in RL and causal inference plus architecture investment is the gating factor that separates leaders from followers.
